title: Pad Thai / Noodle Sauce on Cotton
timeEstimate: 20–60 minutes; 4-component combo (sugar + protein + oil + tannin), order matters
stainChemistry: Pad Thai / noodle sauce is a four-component combo: tamarind (tannin), fish sauce (protein and salt), sugar (caramelizes under heat into a sticky lock), peanut oil and chili oil (fat-soluble carriers for any pigments). The sugar is the trap — it's a sticky matrix that holds everything else against the cotton, and heat caramelizes it into a near-permanent sticky residue. Order matters: cold flush sugar FIRST → cool enzyme for protein → Dawn for oil → vinegar for tannin → peroxide on white for residual color. Recurring traps: hot water (caramelizes sugar + cooks protein + sets tannin all at once), alkaline cleaners as first step (darken tannin permanently), and skipping the Dawn oil-strip (peanut/chili oil holds residual color). Cotton has the widest home bench but order discipline matters most on this stain.
whyThisWorks: Long cold flush dissolves sugar before it caramelizes. Cool enzyme pretreat handles the fish-sauce protein at cool temperature. Dawn strips the peanut and chili oil. Vinegar disrupts the tamarind tannin. 3% peroxide on white cotton finishes residual color. Hot wash AFTER chemistry is safe — protein is gone, oil is stripped, tannin is acid-treated. AIR-DRY first time prevents heat-locking any residual sugar.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): pad-thai is tamarind tannin polyphenols + fish-sauce proteins + sucrose/palm sugar (caramelizes above ~150°C) + peanut oil + chili oil with capsanthin pigment. Pro uses cold flush + protein spotter (cool) + NSD oil-strip + acid-side tannin spotter + 3% H₂O₂ for residual chromophore.
defaultAssumption: Thai food / noodle takeout spill on a cotton shirt or napkin. Fresh = full sequence has a real shot. Hot-dried = often a sticky brown shadow with caramelized sugar. Cotton's widest bench applies.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape solid noodles and sauce off with plastic spoon edge — flat-edge work only, never rub.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold running water, white catch towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hold the BACK of the spot under cold running water 90+ sec — longer than standard because sugar is the lock. Cold only — heat caramelizes sugar AND cooks protein AND sets tannin.

spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean Versatile / Biz / Tide Plus per label, COOL water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dissolve enzyme pretreat in COOL water. Apply to spot, keep damp under a cloth, 20 min. Handles the fish-sauce protein. Cool only — heat damages enzymes AND sets the protein.
Generous Dawn. Soft toothbrush 5–10 min. Strips peanut and chili oil that's holding any pigment. Cool damp-cloth rinse.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1:1 white vinegar + cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Vinegar + cool water on damp cloth, 5 min. Damp-cloth rinse THOROUGHLY before any peroxide.
WHITE cotton: 3% peroxide damp cloth 5–10 min. Hidden test on dyed cotton. NEVER mix with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, hot water (per care label)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hot wash per care label. Safe at this point — sugar flushed, protein digested, oil stripped, tannin acid-treated. Cotton tolerates hot up to 200°F. AIR-DRY first time + inspect.

steps: 2. Long cold water back-flush 90+ sec — dissolve sugar.
3. Enzyme pretreat in COOL water, 20 min — protein FIRST.
4. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 5–10 min — oil strip.
5. White vinegar 1:1 cool 5 min — tannin acid, rinse before peroxide.
6. 3% H₂O₂ damp cloth on white cotton.
7. Hot wash per care label — safe AFTER chemistry. AIR-DRY first time.
products: Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile / Biz / Tide Plus / liquid Protein Spotter)
Cool water for chemistry, hot wash AFTER
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool through chemistry; hot wash AFTER protein/oil cleared
warnings: ORDER MATTERS — sugar flush, then protein, then oil, then tannin acid, then peroxide
NEVER hot water — caramelizes sugar + cooks protein + sets tannin
NEVER alkaline cleaners as first step — DARKEN tannin permanently
NEVER hot dryer with residual sticky sugar — locks permanently
Hidden test on dyed cotton before peroxide
Cotton's widest bench applies AFTER chemistry — hot wash safe at the end

homeSolutions.agent: Four Stains in One — Order Matters
homeSolutions.instruction: Pad Thai sauce is sugar + protein + oil + tannin all at once. The sugar is the trap — it's sticky, holds everything else against the fabric, and caramelizes if any heat touches it. Order: long cold flush (sugar) → cool enzyme (protein) → Dawn (oil) → vinegar (tannin) → 3% peroxide on white cotton (residual color) → hot wash AFTER. Two hard rules: NO hot water (caramelizes sugar + cooks protein + sets tannin all at once) and NO alkaline cleaners as first step (darken tannin permanently).
Scrape solid noodles, peanuts, sauce off with a plastic spoon edge. Flat-edge work only — never rub. Rubbing pushes the sticky sauce into the cotton weave.

homeSolutions.agent: Long Cold Water Back-Flush — Ninety Seconds Plus
homeSolutions.instruction: Turn the cotton inside out. Hold the BACK of the spot under cold running water for at least 90 seconds — longer than most stains because sugar is the lock. Cold only — heat caramelizes the sugar.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme Pretreat — Cool Water, Protein FIRST
homeSolutions.instruction: Dissolve an enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Tide Plus) in COOL water per the label. Apply to the spot, keep damp under a cloth, 20 minutes. The enzyme dismantles the fish-sauce protein. Cool only — heat damages enzymes AND sets the protein.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn Pretreat — Strip Peanut + Chili Oil
homeSolutions.instruction: Generous Dawn dish soap directly on the spot. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 5 to 10 minutes. Cuts the peanut and chili oil that's holding any residual pigment. Cool damp-cloth rinse.
Mix 1 part white vinegar with 1 part cool water. Apply with a damp cloth, press 5 minutes. Disrupts the tamarind tannin grip. Damp-cloth rinse THOROUGHLY before any peroxide.
WHITE cotton: 3% drugstore peroxide on a damp cloth, press 5 to 10 minutes for residual color. Hidden-area test on dyed cotton first.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners — toxic fumes.

homeSolutions.instruction: Hot machine wash per care label. Safe at this point because sugar is flushed, protein is digested, oil is stripped, tannin is acid-treated. Cotton tolerates hot up to 200°F. AIR-DRY first time + inspect before any dryer.

steps: 1. NO hot water. NO alkaline cleaners as first step. NO hot dryer until cleared.
3. Long cold back-flush 90+ sec — dissolve sugar.
4. Enzyme pretreat in COOL water, 20 min — protein FIRST.
5. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 5–10 min — oil strip.
6. Diluted white vinegar 5 min — tannin acid, rinse before peroxide.
7. 3% peroxide damp cloth on white cotton.
8. Hot wash per care label — safe AFTER chemistry. AIR-DRY first time.
products: Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, Tide Plus)
Cool water for chemistry, hot wash AFTER
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot water or hot dryer was used (often locks sugar + cooked protein + set tannin). Stop if alkaline cleaner was used as first step. Stop after 3 home cycles plateau.
warnings: ORDER MATTERS — sugar flush FIRST, protein SECOND, oil THIRD, tannin acid FOURTH, peroxide LAST
NEVER hot water at any pre-wash step
NEVER alkaline cleaners as first step — darken tannin permanently
NEVER hot dryer with sticky residue
Hidden test on dyed cotton before peroxide

neverDo: Never hot water during pre-wash chemistry
Never alkaline cleaners as first step on tannin
Never hot dryer with sticky residue
Never reverse the order — sugar FIRST, then protein, then oil, then acid, then peroxide
Never mix peroxide + chlorine + ammonia + OxiClean
fiberSensitivities: Cotton: widest bench AFTER chemistry — hot wash safe at the end
Dyed cotton: hidden peroxide test mandatory
Cotton blend with polyester: cool only — heat sets oil into polyester even if cotton tolerates it
Cotton blend with elastane: skip chlorine bleach (destroys elastane)

materialWarnings: Order matters more than chemistry choice — sugar / protein / oil / tannin / color in sequence
No hot water during chemistry — caramelizes sugar, cooks protein, sets tannin
Hot wash is finishing-side AFTER chemistry, not setting-side
No alkaline cleaners as first step — darken tannin permanently

escalation.when: Hot water or hot dryer was used early (often locks sugar + cooked protein + set tannin permanently). Alkaline cleaner was used as first step. Cotton blend with polyester (cool-only rules apply). 3 home cycles plateau.
escalation.whatToTell: "Pad Thai / noodle sauce on [white / dyed / blend] cotton, age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [scrape + long cold back-flush + cool enzyme + Dawn + vinegar + 3% peroxide on white + hot wash + air-dry]. Order followed. No hot pre-wash, no alkaline first. [Residual sticky shadow / hot-dried suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with combination-stain (sugar + protein + oil + tannin) chemistry

contextQuestions: Pad Thai, lo mein, drunken noodles, or other Asian sauce?
Has hot water or hot dryer been used?
Has alkaline cleaner been used as first step?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Pad Thai is the four-component combo template — same protocol applies to similar Asian noodle sauces (lo mein, drunken noodles, Singapore noodles). Sugar caramelization is the lock that home users most commonly fail; long cold flush is the unlock. Order discipline matters more than product choice.
